questions.question: Explain the FLP Impossibility Result. Why is it significant for distributed consensus?
keyPoints: States consensus is impossible in asynchronous systems with even one faulty process
Proved by Fischer, Lynch, and Paterson in 1985
Significant because most practical distributed systems are asynchronous
Implies we must make assumptions (synchrony, failure detection) or settle for probabilistic solutions

questions.question: Differentiate between Safety and Liveness properties in consensus protocols.
keyPoints: Safety: Nothing bad happens - no two correct nodes decide different values (consistency)
Liveness: Something good eventually happens - every correct node eventually decides (progress)
Safety prevents incorrectness, Liveness ensures termination
Trade-offs exist - strict safety can reduce liveness

questions.question: What is the Byzantine Generals Problem? How many nodes are required to tolerate f Byzantine failures?
keyPoints: Problem of reaching agreement despite traitorous (Byzantine) generals sending false information
Requires 3f+1 total nodes to tolerate f Byzantine failures
With f faulty nodes, there are still 2f+1 correct nodes forming a majority
Used in blockchain and critical distributed systems

questions.question: Compare 2-Phase Commit and 3-Phase Commit protocols.
keyPoints: 2PC has Prepare and Commit phases - simple but blocking on coordinator failure
3PC adds Pre-commit phase making it non-blocking under certain conditions
3PC assumes no network partition during commit phase
Both ensure atomicity in distributed transactions

questions.question: Explain the CAP theorem and its implications for distributed systems design.
keyPoints: States distributed systems can provide only 2 of 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance
Partition tolerance is mandatory in real systems
During partition: must choose between CP (consistent but unavailable) or AP (available but inconsistent)
Designers must decide based on application requirements

questions.question: What is Leader Election in Raft? Describe the election process.
keyPoints: Raft uses leader to coordinate log replication for simplicity
Followers have election timeouts - if no leader contact, become candidate
Candidates increment term, vote for self, send vote requests to others
If receives majority votes, becomes leader, sends heartbeats to establish authority

questions.question: Explain the three roles in Paxos algorithm and their responsibilities.
keyPoints: Proposers: Initiate consensus by proposing values
Acceptors: Receive proposals, promise to accept, accept values if valid
Learners: Learn the agreed value once accepted by majority
Multi-Paxos extends to handle multiple values with a distinguished proposer

questions.question: Describe the Paxos consensus algorithm in detail. Explain each phase with an example.
keyPoints: Paxos designed to achieve consensus despite failures using Prepare, Accept phases
Prepare: Proposer sends prepare request with proposal number n, Acceptors promise not to accept proposals < n
Accept: Proposer sends accept request with value; Acceptors accept if not promised to higher proposal
If majority accepts, value is chosen/learned
Example: 5 nodes, Proposer P1 sends prepare(1), Acceptors promise, then accepts value v1, majority accepts, v1 chosen
Safety ensured by proposal numbers and promises, Learn phase informs all nodes of decision
Deals with leader changes, failure recovery through these phases

questions.question: Explain Raft consensus algorithm in detail. Include leader election, log replication, and safety mechanisms.
keyPoints: Raft designed for understandability with leader-based approach
Leader Election: Followers timeout, become candidate, increment term, vote, request votes from others. Majority wins election.
Log Replication: Leader receives client request, appends to log, sends AppendEntries to followers, commits when majority acknowledges
Safety: Only leader with majority votes can be elected, logs only committed after majority agreement
Term numbers ensure stale leaders are detected, Log matching property ensures consistency
Heartbeats prevent new elections, membership changes handled carefully

questions.question: Discuss various consensus-related problems in distributed systems. How are they solved?
keyPoints: Consensus: Agreement on single value - solved by Paxos, Raft, PBFT
Atomic Commit: All or nothing - solved by 2PC, 3PC
Byzantine Agreement: Handle malicious failures - requires 3f+1 nodes (PBFT)
Leader Election: Choose coordinator - solved by Raft, Paxos leader
Group Communication: Reliable broadcast - solved by protocols like Reliable Broadcast
Problems arise due to failures, partitions, asynchrony - addressed by quorum, failure detectors, timeouts

questions.question: What is the Split Brain problem? How can it be prevented in distributed systems?
keyPoints: Split brain: Network partition causes multiple nodes to believe they are the leader
Results in conflicting decisions and data inconsistency
Prevention methods: Quorum-based decisions (majority required)
Lease mechanism: Temporary leadership grant with expiration
Fencing: Token or lock that prevents old leader from continuing
Single leader design: Ensure only one leader exists at a time
Used in Raft (majority votes), Paxos (single proposer), distributed databases
